---
title: "Chapter 5 · Dynamic Enterprise Optimization"
subtitle: "Volume II · Dynamic Enterprise Optimization · Week 10"
---

::: {.chapter-meta}
**Volume II, Chapter 5** · Taught in [Week 10](../schedule.html#week-10) · Lab seed `26205`
:::

## Learning Outcome Statements

After completing this chapter, the reader should be able to:

```{=html}
<ol class="los-list"><li>Explain the distinction between static and dynamic enterprise optimization, and identify the declared mechanisms---stocks, delays, feedback, path constraints---that force the dynamic formulation.</li><li>Formulate enterprise optimization problems over continuous time in the canonical Bolza form, identifying state, decision, dynamics, running and terminal value, and path requirements.</li><li>Construct enterprise state trajectories as solutions of declared state-transition equations, and verify the hypotheses under which they exist, are unique, and depend continuously on data.</li><li>Define time-dependent decision variables and policy functions, and distinguish open-loop schedules from feedback rules by their information structure.</li><li>Interpret enterprise dynamics as optimization constraints that couple every instant's decision to every later state.</li><li>Formulate dynamic objective functionals with running and terminal components, discounting, and horizon conventions.</li><li>Analyze intertemporal enterprise trade-offs: investment versus harvest, smoothing versus timing, dips endured for landings.</li><li>Evaluate enterprise evolution under dynamic constraints, including corridors, integral budgets, and terminal targets, and read feasibility as a property of whole trajectories.</li><li>Develop complete dynamic enterprise optimization models for capital, workforce, transformation, innovation, restructuring, and sustainability planning.</li><li>Prepare enterprise models for optimal control: state the formulation contract that Pontryagin's theory (Chapter 6), dynamic programming (Chapter 8), and direct transcription consume.</li></ol>
```

## Reading Guide

Work through the chapter in section order; the full development, proofs, and worked
examples are in the book — this page indexes them and does not replace them.

1. Motivation for Dynamic Enterprise Optimization
2. Enterprise Dynamics
3. Time-Dependent Enterprise States
4. Enterprise Decision Policies
5. Dynamic Objective Functionals
6. Dynamic Enterprise Constraints
7. Enterprise State Trajectories
8. Intertemporal Trade-Offs
9. Dynamic Enterprise Policy Design
10. Computational Considerations
11. Preparation for Optimal Control
12. Chapter Summary
13. Worked Examples
14. Exercises
15. Notes and Sources

## AXIOM Laboratory

<img src="../assets/axiom-logo-small.png" alt="AXIOM" class="axiom-logo-inline"/> This chapter is instrumented by: [AXIOM-04 →](https://axiomdynamics.app/?module=axiom-04) · [AXIOM-05 →](https://axiomdynamics.app/?module=axiom-05)

Launch the module, load the chapter model, modify inputs, run the optimization,
and compare against the worked examples in the book.

## Exercises

12 exercises, grouped **A** concept checks · **B** mathematical ·
**C** computational · **D** enterprise applications. Starred (★) exercises are on the
advanced track. **Full solutions appear in the Instructor's Manual, Chapter 5.**

<h4 class='ex-part'>Part A — Concept checks</h4>

- **Exercise 5.1** — A planning model treats "integration fatigue" as an exogenous quarterly adjustment.
- **Exercise 5.2** — State the composition law (see book) in words and explain why it licenses planning $[0, T]$ in stages.

<h4 class='ex-part'>Part B — Mathematical exercises</h4>

- **Exercise 5.3** — Prove the integral form of Gr\"onwall's inequality used in Theorem (see book)(i): if $\phi(t) \le a + b\int_0^t \phi(s)\, ds$ with $b > 0$, then $\phi(t) \le a e^{bt}$.
- **Exercise 5.4** — For the pipeline system of Example (see book), write $A$ and $B$, compute the eigenvalues and $e^{At}$, and derive the senior-pool response to a unit hiring impulse.
- **Exercise 5.5** — Derive $u^{*}(T, S) = A(T, S)/D(T)$ of Proposition (see book) in closed form (evaluate the integrals), and complete the numeric verification of strict monotonicity in $T$ on $(0, 2.1]$ left to this exercise by the proof.
- **Exercise 5.6** — Construct an instance with a mixed constraint on built capability under which two admissible concentration plans blend to an *inadmissible* plan, completing the caveat of Theorem (see book)(iii).

<h4 class='ex-part'>Part C — Computational exercises</h4>

- **Exercise 5.7** — Prove the differential form of Gr\"onwall's inequality (if $\dot\phi \le b(t)\phi + a(t)$ a.
- **Exercise 5.8** — Prove Jensen's inequality in the integral form used by Proposition (see book)(i), with the equality characterization.

<h4 class='ex-part'>Part D — Enterprise applications</h4>

- **Exercise 5.9** — Implement the capital instance with an error-controlled integrator; reproduce the four policy values of Example (see book) to three decimals and plot Figure (see book)'s trajectories.
- **Exercise 5.10** — Implement gradient-through-simulation for the capital instance: forward simulate, backward accumulate the discrete adjoint, and verify the gradient of $J$ in the mesh decisions against finite differences.
- **Exercise 5.11** — Formulate Meridian's covenant management dynamically: leverage state with declared amortization, covenant band as a state corridor, response schedule as the declared feedback family; audit trajectory-level admissibility across the standing scenario fan and identify the binding quarters.
- **Exercise 5.12 ★** — Extend the admissibility theory to state-constrained instances with viability kernels: formalize the feasibility funnel of Example (see book) as a viability kernel, develop its computation, and prove the monotone shrinkage that the simulation exhibits.

## Downloads

| Resource | File | Seed |
|---|---|---|
| Lecture deck | [`DCT_V2_Ch05_Slides.pptx`](../downloads/DCT_V2_Ch05_Slides.pptx) | — |
| Python laboratory | [Open the laboratory page →](../labs/v2ch05_laboratory.html) · [`DCT_V2_Ch05_Lab.ipynb`](../downloads/DCT_V2_Ch05_Lab.ipynb) | `26205` |
| Excel workbook | [`DCT_V2_Ch05_Lab.xlsx`](../downloads/DCT_V2_Ch05_Lab.xlsx) | `26205` |

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
All three companions consume the same seeded engine (`26205`), so their numbers
agree by construction — the MFMF convention, carried forward.
:::
